chunks.text: a twenty - six - year - old English woman named Jane Goodall walked alone into the forest of Tanzania
in East Africa.
Her aim was to observe wild chimpanzee in their own natural environment.
In those days
it was almost unprecedented for a woman to enter the depth of Africa by herself.
She did not even have a university degree.
But the paleontologist Louis Leakey believed in her passion and patience and supported her.
In the forest of Gombe, she sat for months without showing herself
deep among the trees.
After a while
the chimpanzees stopped fearing her and began to show her glimpses of their daily lives.
Her greatest discovery was the observation that chimpanzees made twigs into a tool for catching termites.
To the world of science, which had until then believed that only humans used tools
this came as a great shock.
Goodall also showed that chimpanzees had emotions similar to those of humans — family bonds
sadness, and conflict.
Her observation rewrote, from the very ground, the line between humanity and other animals.
In her later years, Goodall devoted herself to the conservation of wildlife.
The youth program she founded, called Roots and Shoots
has become a starting point for young people in over sixty countries to take on environmental issues in their own neighborhoods.
" Hope lies in action.
" Her words remain, even now
a steady encouragement for the many youth who carry concern for the future.
